coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of a fluorescence optical probe for Fe3+ based on Barberry derived carbon quantum dots: Deep insight on interaction from experimental and theoretical approach
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammadnejad, Masoumeh
Khatibi pour, Fatemeh
Ghiasi, Mina
coredata.dc:description: A green facile method was developed to synthesize the carbon quantum dots from barberry, a native plant, as a new carbon source. The synthesis strategy is a simple one-step hydrothermal process without requiring hazardous chemical reagents. The spherical structure of b-CDs with an average particle size of 3.3 nm including oxygen and nitrogen functional groups was evaluated by various analyses (FT-IR, DLS, XRD, FE-SEM, TEM, AFM). The synthesized b-CDs with QY of 12.82 % were used as a fluorescence optical probe (λex = 350 nm, λem = 450 nm) to measure iron ions as an inorganic pollutant. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of b-CDs decreases due to the interaction between the functional groups on the surface of b-CDs and Fe3+. The linear relationship was in the concentration range from 5.00 × 10−6 to 8.00 × 10−5 M with LOD of 1.73 × 10−6 M which was lower than the concentration determined by WHO. AGREE software was utilized to prove the greenness of the reported analytical method. In parallel, the theoretical calculations employing Density Functional Theory (DFT) were used to understand the electronic structure and atomic level insight into the functionalized b-CDs and interaction of Fe3+ with the different sites of b-CDs surface. From a thermodynamic point of view, the interaction of Fe3+ from the COOH functional group in b-CDs is more favorable than other sites with more negative Gibbs free energy interaction. Overall, producing eco-friendly fluorescence nanomaterials with favorable fluorescence properties, low cost, and sufficient sensitivity like b-CDs seems promising for designing new fluorescence probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Green synthesis
Barberry
Fluorescence quenching
Inorganic pollutants
DFT calculation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of b-CDs hydrothermal synthesis conditions
Dependence of b-CDs fluorescence intensity on the excitation wavelength (λex)
Optimization of effective parameters on b-CDs fluorescence intensity
pH effect on fluorescence emission of b-CDs
Fe3+ metal ion determination using b-CDs optical probe
Greenness evaluation of analysis method via user-friendly AGREE software
Structural optimization of b-CDs and HOMO-LUMO analysis
Possible interaction mechanism between b-CDs and Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bar chart of b-CDs fluorescence intensity in various hydrothermal condition.

(a) FT-IR spectrum (b) XRD pattern (c) EDAX spectrum of the b-CDs in line scan mode (d–g) Elemental mapping of b-CDs. (d–f) Individual elemental distribution (purple for Carbon, red for Oxygen, and green for Nitrogen), and (g) Combined state.
(a) DLS spectrum (size distribution by frequency) of aqueous suspension (b) AFM image (c) FE-SEM images (d, e) TEM images of b-CDs at different magnifications at d) 250 nm e) 100 nm.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and photographs of I) diluted II) concentrated b-CDs solution under visible light (Inside); (b) PL spectra of b-CDs and Barberry; photograph of b-CDs excited by a 365 nm UV lamp (Inside); (c) PL spectra of b-CDs synthesized at various excitation wavelengths; (d) Normalized PL spectra of b-CDs.
(a) Effect of pH (b) concentration on b-CDs fluorescence intensity (c) Fluorescence stability of b-CDs over storage time.

(a) Fluorescence quenching spectra of b-CDs solution in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0.00–5 × 10−4 M) at pH≈ 3.5 (b) Linear relationship between (F0–F = ΔF) and Fe3+ concentration from 5.00 × 10−6 to 8.00 × 10−5 M. (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of b-CDs at λex ∼350 nm in the presence and absence of Fe3+ ion, respectively).
Generic result of greenness assessment (left) and the corresponding color scale and guide for reference (right).

(a): Top view and side view of optimized geometry of b-CDs model with some structural details, (b): Surface plots of frontier molecular orbital (FMOs) of b-CDs; the values depicted are the energy in eV with the corresponding HOMO-LUMO energy gap (down), (c) The model used for b-CDs with numbering for some key atoms.
Optimized structure of b-CDs/Fe3+ complex with relative stability energy (kcal/mol).
Schematic of the synthesis steps of Barberry-derived carbon quantum dots (b-CDs).
Data of EDAX analysis of b-CDs in line scan mode.
Recovery values for analysis of real sample using b-CDs fluorescence probe and atomic absorption spectrometer (n = 3).
Overview of natural precursor derived CDs: hydrothermal synthesis status, characterizations and Fe3+ assay.
Calculated net atomic charge using NBO calculations.
Calculated thermodynamic functions (kcal/mol) of complexation between Fe3+ and b-CDs from different positions in solvent phase.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of a fluorescence optical probe for Fe3+ based on Barberry derived carbon quantum dots: Deep insight on interaction from experimental and theoretical approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry Faculty of Chemistry Alzahra University Tehran Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry and Nano Chemistry Faculty of Chemistry Alzahra University Tehran Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physical Chemistry and Nano Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physical Chemistry and Nano Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green facile method was developed to synthesize the carbon quantum dots from barberry, a native plant, as a new carbon source. The synthesis strategy is a simple one-step hydrothermal process without requiring hazardous chemical reagents. The spherical structure of b-CDs with an average particle size of 3.3 nm including oxygen and nitrogen functional groups was evaluated by various analyses (FT-IR, DLS, XRD, FE-SEM, TEM, AFM). The synthesized b-CDs with QY of 12.82 % were used as a fluorescence optical probe (λex = 350 nm, λem = 450 nm) to measure iron ions as an inorganic pollutant. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of b-CDs decreases due to the interaction between the functional groups on the surface of b-CDs and Fe3+. The linear relationship was in the concentration range from 5.00 × 10−6 to 8.00 × 10−5 M with LOD of 1.73 × 10−6 M which was lower than the concentration determined by WHO. AGREE software was utilized to prove the greenness of the reported analytical method. In parallel, the theoretical calculations employing Density Functional Theory (DFT) were used to understand the electronic structure and atomic level insight into the functionalized b-CDs and interaction of Fe3+ with the different sites of b-CDs surface. From a thermodynamic point of view, the interaction of Fe3+ from the COOH functional group in b-CDs is more favorable than other sites with more negative Gibbs free energy interaction. Overall, producing eco-friendly fluorescence nanomaterials with favorable fluorescence properties, low cost, and sufficient sensitivity like b-CDs seems promising for designing new fluorescence probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article. No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been recognized as new carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials in the past decade due to unprecedented optical properties. Sun et al. synthesized a fluorescent carbon nanoparticle (less than 10 nm), and called it “carbon dot” [1]. The discovery of CDs led to intensive research on this fascinating carbon nanomaterial with extraordinary potential. The surface of CDs is oxidized by a large number of hydroxyl (–OH), carboxyl (–COOH), and amine (–NH2) functional groups along with sp3 hydrocarbons [2–4]. One obvious superiority of CDs is that the carbon-like core and organic functional groups on their surface all together determine their nature and characteristics [5]. The remarkable features of CDs are impressive photoluminescence [6], resistance to photobleaching [7], high aqueous dispersibility [4], non-toxicity [8], high chemical stability [9], good biocompatibility [10,11], cost-effective synthesis [9], easy surface modification [12], chemical inertness [11] and so on; Therefore, these unique properties provide a wide range of applications in various fields such as nanomedicine [13], bioimaging [3,6], drug delivery [3,10], biological labeling [10], photocatalysis [10,6], photovoltaic devices [10] and sensing of inorganic and organic pollutants as optical probe [6,14]. So, the synthesis of these compounds can be developed for different applications.

Synthesis methods of CDs can be divided into two main routes, i.e., (1) the top-down route such as arc discharge [15], electrochemical oxidation [10], laser ablation [4,15] etc. (2) the bottom-up route including pyrolysis [10], hydrothermal/ solvothermal [4], ultrasonic [16], and microwave-assisted method [4,17]. In general, bottom-up approaches are preferable; among them, the hydrothermal or simple heating method is widely accepted for the synthesis of CDs. The attractive features of a simple preparation process, high efficiency, low energy consumption, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness make it a popular method for preparing CDs and provide the possibility of easy control over reactions[18,19].

Over the past few years, various chemical precursors such as citric acid [20], diethylene glycol [21], urea [12], thiourea [22], benzene [23], phytic acid [24], ammonium citrate [21], and phenylenediamine [25] been used as raw materials for synthesizing CDs. However, the cost of these raw materials is expensive and not economical, and on the other hand, will cause environmental issues due to chemical toxicity during production. To this end, an eco-friendly approach to improve the synthesis of CDs and to overcome the limitations of chemical precursors is the use of green precursors, which caused more attention to the safe preparation of CDs, named “green CDs” [26]. The term “green precursors” refers to materials that either exist naturally or are derived from renewable natural products. Among green carbon precursors, different parts of plants are preferred due to less exposure to the diversity of green precursor compositions with different origins [27]. Therefore, in this study, Barberry, as a subtropical native plant located in South Khorasan province in Iran, was considered as a new source of carbon because of its abundance, availability, and cost-effectiveness. For CDs hydrothermal synthesis, various chemical compounds of barberry (i.e., pectin, vitamin C, iso-quinoline alkaloid, minerals) are safe based on green chemistry.

By adopting the twelve basic principles of green chemistry, the use or production of hazardous substances during the synthesis process can be avoided [10,28]. At a glance, these principles are multifaceted and suggest eco-friendly techniques to reduce the hazards of solvents and non-generation of residues as waste, use of cheap, renewable, abundant resources, and potential for scale-up [29,30].

In addition, we took profit of the excellent fluorescence characteristic of CDs synthesized from Barberry to consider its application as an optical probe for the determination of iron (Fe3+) as a heavy metal ion. Iron is one of the essential metal ions in living biosystems. Among the two oxidation states of ferrous (II) and ferric (III), Fe (III) is more harmful due to its insolubility and production of toxic radicals. Excess accumulation and deficiency of Fe (III) in the human body can lead to various diseases, such as Parkinson’s, Alzheimer’s, hemochromatosis, liver and kidney damage, iron-deficiency anemia, hemolytic and heart failure [28,31,32]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the maximum permissible limit of Fe (III) in drinking water is 5.36 µM [10]. Therefore, measuring iron is important to evaluate the quality of drinking water and prevent the occurrence of these disorders.

Finally, after characterizing the prepared b-CDs and determining the metal ion of iron, to realize the details of the interaction between the b-CDs and metal ions, the DFT calculations were also performed to gain electronic structure and atomic level insight into the interactions of Fe3+ with the different sites and functional groups on the b-CDs surface.
Dried Barberry was bought from a local grocery shop (Tehran, Iran). All the metal ions were of analytical grade from Sigma-Aldrich Company (St. Louis, MO, USA), which is mentioned in Section S1a.
For analytical characterization, all the devices used are listed with details including the brand and model of instruments in Section S1b.

The steps of hydrothermal synthesis of CDs derived from Iranian Barberry are explained in detail in Section S2. Also, in this Section, the purification process of prepared b-CDs is mentioned. The general schematic of the synthesis procedure of b-CDs is shown below (see Scheme 1 ).

Quinine sulfate (QY = 54 %) was selected as a reference standard based on λem of b-CDs [33]. A reference solution of quinine sulfate was made in 0.1 M H2SO4. In this regard, solutions of quinine sulfate and b-CDs with absorbance less than 0.1 were prepared, including 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, and 0.08. The absorption and integrated fluorescence intensities of quinine sulfate and b-CDs were measured under the same conditions with λex = 350 nm. Finally, the quantum yield of b-CDs was calculated using the following equation:
φ X
=
φ
std
I X
A X
A
std
I
std
η X
η
std
2
Where
φ , A , I , a n d
η
are the quantum yield, absorption, integrated fluorescence and refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscript “std” and “x” refer to the corresponding parameter of the fluorescent standard and b-CDs sample.

In this study, a graphitic fragment substituted with nitrogen atoms and oxygen-containing groups (hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl groups) at the edge (C26H14N2O6) was utilized as the model for b-CDs. All calculations were based on Density Functional Theory (DFT) employing the B3LYP exchange–correlation function [34] using the 6-311/+G** standard basis set. All calculations were carried out using the Gaussian 09 program package [53] and the molecular geometry was designed using the GaussView 5. The comprehensive explanations are mentioned in Section (S3) of the Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of b-CDs hydrothermal synthesis conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The method of hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots includes the carbonization process. Since carbonization is an endothermic process, temperature is a key factor in the synthesis. The temperature can vary in a wide range from 90–200 °C. Low temperature may prevent complete carbonization of the carbon source into carbon quantum dots; hence, it leads to weak fluorescence emission. Meanwhile, very high temperature destroys the surface structure of carbon quantum dots due to excessive oxidation of the initial precursor [4,35].

At first, the temperature was fixed at 150 °C with the reference [36] and the reaction time was changed as a single variable (12 h, 2 h, 1 h, and 30 min), and the fluorescence intensity of the product was recorded. By comparing the maximum fluorescence intensity, the optimal duration was considered to be 1 h (Fig. 1 ), which is very low time and can be introduced as an efficient and speed synthetic route. Then, it was decided to bring the reaction temperature below 150 °C i.e., 125 °C at the optimal time. Weak fluorescence intensity was observed at lower temperature; As a result, the optimal synthesis temperature was selected as 150 °C. The reproducibility of green b-CDs synthesis is given in Section (S4).

Several characterization techniques such as FT-IR, XRD, DLS, EDAX, FE-SEM, TEM, and AFM were utilized to investigate the structural properties and morphology of b-CDs. Besides that, the optical properties of b-CDs were evaluated using UV–Vis absorption spectra and spectrofluorometric analysis [37].

Dispersion of functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen on the aromatic rings of the b-CDs structure, followed by excellent solubility in water, was proved by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 a). The crystallinity nature of b-CDs was described by evaluating the XRD pattern (Fig. 2b). The chemical composition related to the main elements of b-CDs structure (C, O, and N) and their uniform distribution on the surface was checked by EDAX analysis in line scan and mapping mode, as represented in Fig. 2c–g. The amount of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements are reported as 60.13, 28.12, and 11.74 %W respectively in Table 1 . To assess the morphology and size distribution of b-CDs particles, several microscopic imaging techniques such as FE-SEM, TEM, followed DLS, AFM analysis were applied in varied magnifications (Fig. 3 ). The results demonstrated that b-CDs are mono-dispersed without any obvious aggregation and the average diameter of spherical particles with surface defects is approximately 3.3 nm. All supplementary content related to b-CDs structural characterization is detailed in Section S5 based on references.

Carbon quantum dots are known to exhibit special optical behavior. Carbon quantum dots have broad and strong absorption band in the UV and a tail towards the visible region. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the UV–Vis spectra of the synthesized b-CDs shows two different peaks at 222 nm and 287 nm. The sharp absorption peak at 222 nm could be attributed to the π-π* electronic transition of the C=C aromatic bond in the sp2 hybridized domain of the graphite core [11,38,20]; While a peak around 287 nm is related to the n–π* transition of the C=O, C–N, C–OH bonds in the sp3-hybridized domain due to hydroxyl (–OH), carboxyl (–COO) or amine (–NH2) functional groups with lone pairs of electrons on the surface of b-CDs [3,39].
The appearance of liquid b-CDs is a brown-yellowish color under visible light; when exposed to UV irradiation at a wavelength of 365 nm, bright blue luminescence is shown.

The fluorescence study of carbon quantum dots not only provides the possibility of evaluating the uniformity of their chemical properties, but also determines whether the fluorescence emission is dependent on the excitation wavelength or not. The fluorescence spectra of the diluted aqueous solution of the initial carbon precursor (Barberry) and b-CDs solution were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

According to the spectrum (Fig. 4b), a sharp fluorescence peak of b-CDs was appeared at the emission wavelength of 450 nm that confirmed the fluorescence property of b-CDs, but the solution of barberry precursor does not show fluorescence by itself. Therefore, it can be concluded that the carbon precursor of barberry is transformed into carbon quantum dots (b-CDs) by being exposed to hydrothermal conditions (high temperature and steam pressure), which shows that the synthesis has been done correctly.

To calculate the b-CDs quantum yield, quinine sulfate salt was chosen as the standard reference considering the excitation and emission wavelength of b-CDs. The quantum yield of the synthesized b-CDs was determined to be 12.82 %, as described, indicating favorable fluorescence characteristic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dependence of b-CDs fluorescence intensity on the excitation wavelength (λex)
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the dependence of b-CDs fluorescence emission on the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity was recorded by changing the excitation wavelength [3]. It is noticeable that when λex was enhanced from 280 to 350 nm, a significant increase in fluorescence intensity was seen, which finally reached its maximum intensity. By changing the λex from 350 to 440 nm, a clear decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed, as shown in Fig. 4c, d. Therefore, an excitation wavelength of 350 nm was selected for further studies. It should also be remarked that when the excitation wavelength increases, a red shift occurs in the emission wavelength [31,35,26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of effective parameters on b-CDs fluorescence intensity
pH effect on fluorescence emission of b-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 10.00 µL of b-CDs original solution was diluted to 5 mL in different pH values from 1 to 12. pH values were adjusted by adding the aqueous solution of HCl or NaOH (0.10 M). The fluorescence spectra of these solutions were recorded. Fig. 5 a shows that the maximum fluorescence intensity of b-CDs solution is at pH = 3.5. Due to the acidic nature of the precursor used, the fluorescence stability of b-CDs probe were effectively maintained in acidic conditions. On the other hand, at pH 3.5, the concentration of [OH−] is low in the medium and does not lead to the formation of Fe(OH)3 precipitate, resulting Fe3+ exist as free ions or soluble nitrate form [40].

To find the optimal value of b-CDs, different volumes from 0.50 to 80.00 µl of b-CDs were diluted by deionized water (the final volume was 2.00 ml). Fluorescence intensity was measured in the mentioned volume range at pH = 3.5. The maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained at 0.70 % V/V, as seen in Fig. 5b.

One of the important parameters in expressing the quality of synthesis and persistence of the fluorescent feature of b-CDs is to check the stability of its fluorescence over time [11,41]. In this regard, the solution was kept in a dark glass container after the first synthesis of b-CDs. Its fluorescence intensity was checked at the λex of 350 nm and the λem of 450 nm for 4 months. The fluorescence intensity of b-CDs was compared with its freshly prepared solution after the mentioned time. According to the bar chart (Fig. 5c), 55 % of its initial fluorescence intensity has been maintained up to 4 months. Additionally, stability was evaluated by not observing precipitation or changing the color of the solution.

It seems that the raw materials used in the synthesis of carbon quantum dots affect their fluorescence properties. For example, carbon quantum dots synthesized from pineapples are completely degraded after several weeks of storage. Besides, the fungi appeared on the surfaces of pineapple-derived carbon quantum dots [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ metal ion determination using b-CDs optical probe
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the application of b-CDs optical probe, its sensing properties were studied in the presence of Fe3+. In this manner, different volumes of the initial concentration of Fe3+ ion (1.00 × 10−3 M) were added to the b-CDs solution under optimal conditions (pH = 3.5, %V/V = 0.70). The fluorescence spectrum was immediately recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The trend of fluorescence variation of b-CDs optical probe in the presence of Fe3+ is shown in Fig. 6 a. By increasing the concentration of Fe3+ analyte in the range of 1.00 × 10−6 to 5.00 × 10−4 M, a significant decrease in the initial fluorescence intensity of b-CDs (F0) was observed.

Furthermore, the calibration curve was drawn based on the difference in fluorescence intensity of b-CDs optical probe before and after (ΔF) adding Fe3+ versus its concentration. As seen in Fig. 6b, there is a linear relationship in the concentration range from 5.00 × 10−6 to 8.00 × 10−5 M, with LOD of 1.73 × 10−6 M based on S/N of 3. Taken together, the experimental results led to a promising fluorescence method of Fe3+ determination using b-CDs probe.

On the other hand, the obtained experimental data were in agreement with the Stern-Volmer relationship (F/F0 = 1 + KSV [Q] = 1 + kqτ0[Q]) [42], as mentioned in Section (S6.a). The selectivity of the spectrofluorimetric analysis method towards Fe3+ in the presence of different metal ions as interferences is given in Section (S6.b). To evaluate the repeatability and reproducibility, the response of b-CDs optical probe was reported with RSD of 3.00 % and 5.29 %, respectively in (S6.c) chart.

To demonstrate the efficiency of the b-CDs fluorescence probe in real sample, tap water (Tehran, Alzahra University) was tested. A standard solution of Fe3+ with concentrations of 20, 40, 50, 60, and 70 µM was spiked in each tap water containing b-CDs solution, and the fluorescence spectra before and after the spike of the standard solution were recorded under optimal conditions. The recovery and RSD% were calculated and reported in Table 2 . As depicted, the recovery values were in the range of 83.33–106.34 % (using the roposed methodology) and 92.50–103.00 % (atomic absorption spectrometer), respectively. The RSD found to be 1.36–5.56 % in tap water, which accepted the applicability of the proposed method.

As a summary of the hydrothermal synthesis conditions along with the optical and structural characteristics, a comprehensive Table 3 is given. By comparison, it is concluded that the successful synthesis of carbon quantum dots was done in the shortest possible time by hydrothermal treatment in this project. In terms of structural and optical characteristics, the prepared b-CDs have smaller particle sizes and desirable quantum yield compared to other carbon dots derived from chemical and natural precursors, as reported in references. The Fe3+ assay parameters are acceptable; most importantly, the detection limit is lower than the permissible limit determined based on WHO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Greenness evaluation of analysis method via user-friendly AGREE software
ce:section.ce:para: Numerous criteria, including values and toxicity of reagent, the number of procedural steps, energy consumption, automated and miniaturized method, operator’ safety, and produced waste are considered to evaluate an analytical methodology’s greenness; For this reason, the main focus of Green Analytical Chemistry (GAC) is to create safe analytical procedure for humans and the environment [43–45]. Applying specific criteria to measure the grade of method greenness requires computational tools, which we recommend the Analytical GREEnness calculator [46]. The Analytical GREEnness calculator is a user-friendly tool designed based on the 12 principles of green analytical chemistry; each of the principles is briefly mentioned below.

The analytical method proposed in this article is spectrofluorometry based on b-CDs optical probe fluorescence to determine Fe3+. This procedure is considered as a non-invasive method without sample treatment (principle 1), and the minimum sample required for analysis is 0.01 ml (p 2). The analytical device is placed in off-line mode (p 3), and the number of distinct analytical steps is 3 (p 4). This manual method has no sample preparation (p 5). No derivative agent is applied (p 6), the total amount of produced waste is 60.00 mL including b-CDs, water solvent and analyte solution (p 7), one analyte determined in a single run and the sample throughput is ∼45 samples per hour (p 8). Spectrofluorometry is an analytical technique with low energy consumption (<0.1 kWh) (p 9). All reagents are renewable (p 10), the method involves no toxic reagent or solvents (p 11), and finally, no danger threatens the operator’s safety (p 12).
As it is clear in the pictogram (Fig. 7 ), the last score is equal to 0.78, that the green color represents the greenness of analytical method in this article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural optimization of b-CDs and HOMO-LUMO analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The model of b-CDs with oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups at the edge (C26H14N2O6), Fig. 8 c, was fully optimized at B3LYP/6-311+G** level of calculations with no initial symmetry restrictions. In the following the fully optimized b-CDs geometry in gas phase re-optimized by considering the solvent effect using PCM method in water solvent. The calculated results indicate that the b-CDs stabilized by about 29.43 kcal/mol in water solvent. Fig. 8a shows the optimized structure and some structural details of b-CDs model in the water phase, the little planner distorted geometry caused by edge functionalization. Calculation of vibrational frequencies has confirmed stationary point with no negative eigenvalue observed in the force constant matrix.

The average distance between C and C atoms in b-CDs model is 1.97 Å that is in good agreement with X-ray crystallography data, 2.06 Å which validates our computational models. The DFT computed surface plots of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), together called the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) are presented in Fig. 8b. Furthermore, the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO is important for analyzing molecular electrical transport properties. The value of the energy gap for b-CDs is equal to 1.90 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible interaction mechanism between b-CDs and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
To further study the interactions between b-CDs and Fe3+ ion, geometric optimization, interaction energies calculations (I.E.) and all thermodynamic functions including
Δ G
I n t
°
,
Δ H
I n t
°
and
Δ S
I n t
°
were calculated for different b-CDs/Fe3+ complexes.

In the first step the NBO analysis were performed to calculate the net atomic charges, Table 4 . As the calculated results indicate the most negative charge is on nitrogen atom of amine group, N1 and N2 (−0.79), oxygen atom of hydroxyl group, O3 and O5 (−0.71) and oxygen atom of carboxylic group, O2 and O6 (−0.61) and O1 and O4 (−0.67) of b-CDs; consequently, these atoms have more tendency to interact with Fe3+ ion.

Since three different spin states are possible for Fe3+ ion, this cation was optimized in high spin (S = 5/2), intermediate spin state (S = 3/2) and low spin (S = 1/2) state. The results of calculations indicate that the high spin state is about 87.45 and 145.36 kcal/mol more stable that intermediate and low spin state respectively. In continue, Fe3+ ion was placed on the basal plane of the carbon quantum dot sheet from different positions to interact with functional groups at the edge of b-CDs. Fig. 9 illustrate the optimized geometry of b-CDs/Fe3+ complexes in the solvent phase. From Table 5 , the I.E. values between different functional groups of b-CDs and Fe3+ ion can be ranked as follows: carboxyl > hydroxyl > amine. The more negative of the I.E. value indicate the stronger intermolecular interaction and more stable complex.

All thermochemical functions of the interaction between Fe3+ and b-CDs in solvent phase were calculated at B3LYP/6-311+G** level and presented in Table 5. The analysis of the calculated
Δ H
I n t
°
and
Δ G
I n t
°
in Table 5 shows that the complex formation of b-CDs/Fe3+ in the solvent phase from three different positions are exothermic and spontaneous, (negative value of
Δ H
I n t
°
and
Δ G
I n t
°
). However, the more negative value of
Δ G
I n t
°
(−37.37 kcal/mol), from COOH functional group rather than other positions indicate that the Fe3+ prefers to interact with b-CDs from this position. Furthermore, the stability of b-CDs/Fe3+ complex from COOH functional group comes from two interaction sites (C=O and C–OH) rather than OH and NH2 functional groups. Moreover, the negative values of entropy related to complex formation from two reactants b-CDs and Fe3+ and production of b-CDs/Fe3+ complex. Furthermore, the NBO analysis of three different complexes show that the net atomic charge on Fe3+ ion in b-CDs/Fe3+ complexes reduced to 1.89, 1.91 and 2.02 values while the Fe3+ ion interact with COOH, OH and NH2 functional group in b-CDs respectively that show electrostatic interaction. Consequently, according to experimental results the fluorescence quenching of b-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions could be explained by two electrostatic interactions of Fe3+ ion with b-CDs from COOH functional group spontaneously from thermodynamic point of view with
Δ G
I n t
°
= −37.37 kcal/mol that provides a platform specific and selective sensing by monitoring the fluorescence intensity changes of b-CDs.

In summary, we herein have demonstrated that a facile, efficient, cost-effective, and environmentally green hydrothermal method could be used to synthesize highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots using Barberry as an herbal carbon source. By using this precursor, the obvious advantages of green chemistry can be achieved, including reducing exposure to chemicals, using cheap, renewable, and abundant raw materials, reducing waste, and the scale-up potential in the synthesis of carbon quantum dots. This helps chemists protect the environment by expanding green chemistry. The greenness degree of the b-CDs synthesis was determined using AGREE software. The obtained b-CDs exhibit superior optical properties such as high fluorescence intensity with 12.82 % QY, excellent stability, good resistance to photobleaching, and excitation-dependent behavior. Further, structural characterization techniques (FE-SEM, TEM, DLS, AFM) were performed, which revealed a uniform distribution of spherical particles without aggregation with an average particle size of 3.3 nm. Also, unlike carbon as the main component of b-CDs, which indicates poor solubility in water, b-CDs have good dispersibility in water due to carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino functional groups, paving the way for their utilization in various applications. These b-CDs as optical probe with favorable features have selective ion detection capability. Fluorescence quenching of b-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions provides a platform for their quantitative sensing by monitoring the fluorescence intensity changes of b-CDs. Indeed, the fluorescence of b-CDs was quenched with increasing Fe3+ concentration, and under the optimum conditions, Fe3+ could be determined in the linear dynamic range of 5.00 × 10−6 to 8.00 × 10−5 M with a detection limit of 1.73 × 10−6 M and a good correlation (R2 = 0.992). The applicability of the b-CDs-based fluorescence probe was verified in real sample and compared with the standard AAS method. Moreover, DFT calculations have been carried out to study the interaction between b-CDs and Fe3+, in the solvent from a thermodynamic viewpoint. The results of calculations indicate that interaction between b-CDs and Fe3+ is exothermic and spontaneously. Furthermore, the COOH functional group is more suitable than the other sites to interact with iron cation. It is expected that carbon quantum dots prepared from barberry residue will be widely used in various fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Research Council of Alzahra University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125775.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
